paragraphs.body: I gripped the flimsy $5 dollar bill in my hand tightly as my mom passed it over. I was helping her around the house doing chores. For helping her out she gave me money. I never had my own money before, so I spent my time examining the rumpled worn paper. It felt like I was holding endless possibilities in my hand as the overpowering mix of metal, ink, and paper flowed into my nose. As my eyes were staring into Abraham Lincoln's crinkled eyes my mom interrupted my moment by telling me to look at her and listen. I lifted my gaze into her dull blue eyes, noting the crows feet surrounding her eyes as she began talking. She was telling me how to treat my money, explaining to cherish and treat money with respect and that it will benefit me in the long run. That confused me, why do I need to treat a $5 dollar bill with respect? It’s not like it is human.

annotations.text: I never had my own money before, so I spent my time examining the rumpled worn paper.
annotations.comment: What a great moment to narrate! The first of your own money you ever held in your hand :)

annotations.text: That confused me, why do I need to treat a $5 dollar bill with respect? It’s not like it is human.

paragraphs.body: More often than not I am pondering about how I got to where I am right now in my life. Thinking about all I endured in just 18 years of living and the values I carry with me as a young adult. What exactly shaped me? I mean all of my experiences and what I learned from them. It curated who I wanted to be as a person and just what I want in general.

Waking up to the sun hitting my eyes forced me to squint because of the brightness. My ears perked up as I heard loud voices travelling through the vents into my baby pink room, and I suddenly remembered I needed to go tell my parents about my shoes ripping while playing “gymnastic tag” the day before. I was excited because I really wanted this new pair of sketchers everyone at school had, so I threw my comforter off and sprinted into the bathroom to brush my teeth. I spit the bubblegum flavored foam into the drain and left my princess toothbrush by the sink, still in my minion-themed pj’s as I descended the stairs. When I was almost at the bottom, the loud voices boomed even louder saying incoherent words and I still remember I gripped the banister making my knuckles turn white as I tried to listen in on my parents. I then crept onto the cold tile floor toward the sound of the voices in the kitchen. As I got closer, I plastered myself against the green wall leading to the kitchen and settled under the lightswitch to hear the conversation better. I heard my dad yell,” Lisa! If you spend any more money we are going to be out on the streets. We have three kids and you want to waste my hard earned money on pointless items!” My mom quickly shot back a slurred sentence I didn’t make out. I remember my palms becoming clammy with sweat and my heart beating harder and faster. I turned on my heel as heat flushed my face and tears started brimming in the backs of my eyes about to spill over as I quickly booked it up the steps toward my room. I was stopped at the top by my older sister asking what was wrong and I couldn’t hold the tears in any longer. She told me to stop being such a baby, so I rushed into my room and shut and clicked the lock on the door, planting face first into my comforter. I kept thinking about how selfish I was for thinking about asking for new sneakers. The salty tears covered my lips, and I heard Megan's voice downstairs then footsteps came to my door. A soft knock followed by my dads voice asking me to open the door to talk, so I sniffled and pushed down the handle to release the lock. My dad asked again for me to unlock the door and I said I had, but then I realized I messed up again because my bedroom lock had been a problem and I wasn’t supposed to lock it until it was fixed. I placed my head in the palms of my hands and mouthed an apology over and over. My mom removed the door handle and got the door open and my parents came in and asked me what was wrong. I lied and said Megan calling me baby hurt my feelings because I didn’t want them to think I was selfish for wanting new shoes and I didn’t want to be the reason why we would live on the streets because of wanting shoes. After I calmed down I decided to not ask for anything from them to make sure our family could stay together.

annotations.text: She told me to stop being such a baby, so I rushed into my room and shut and clicked the lock on the door, planting face first into my comforter. I kept thinking about how selfish I was for thinking about asking for new sneakers.
After I calmed down I decided to not ask for anything from them to make sure our family could stay together.

paragraphs.body: Want is the underlying ache that leads our lives. A show of what we don’t have as well as the potential of who and what we can be. Want is a pulsating desire that sits inside waiting to be ignited and brought to life by choices. If you are lost on what you are supposed to do you follow that want and it will lead you to your goals. The range of want is infinite and is why you are where you are today. Your wants have shaped who you are from a young age. Making a choice based on what you want changes who you are slowly. Wanting to go to a certain college from a young age has led you to instead of hanging out with friends one night to instead study which in turn taught you discipline. Funny, but it is under just about every aspect of who you are and what your life is like. Want is good and can also be bad. In the end, whether it led you to something good or bad, your wants have helped shape you and your choices. Looking back I find it intriguing that my want to help my family financially even though I wasn’t aware of how money worked at the time changed me at that moment. I learned the value of money and how to be financially smart as time went on, and I also became aware of how my choices affect others. That moment made me so selfless that I still feel guilt for wanting anything and putting others out.

annotations.text: Want is the underlying ache that leads our lives.
annotations.comment: This is a beautiful line and transition!

paragraphs.body: Money gives you boundless opportunities. A flimsy green piece of paper provides all sorts of security and opportunities. I always make sure I carry cash with me so I have security on the off chance I need it. Knowing a tightly wrapped bundle of cash rests in whatever bag I have puts my mind at ease. The scent of ink and paper that coats my fingers after handling the bills to ensure I have the correct amount. Being prepared for anything to happen is something I always ensure I can do. The sound of possibilities that hums in the air when the cash is in my tight grasp knowing I have the ability to do what I want.

annotations.text: flimsy green piece of paper provides all sorts of security and opportunities.
annotations.comment: Aha! Is it about security? Safety?

paragraphs.body: Money is like people. It can be destructive for you or beneficial. People come and people go, and that is just how it is. There are tons of reasons why you meet and know the people you do, whether they stick with you forever, or they are just a part of your life for a little while. Coming to terms with that fact makes it easier to understand you can’t hold onto your relationship with somebody forever. Cherishing the time you had with those individuals and respecting that you and that person aren’t meant for eachother anymore makes living for you a lot easier. Spending all your time and energy on others and holding onto a relationship that is over doesn’t help you to achieve what you want to do and who you want to be. I’d like to think I'm very wise and mature for a college freshman and that all the choices made in my life have been motivated by everything I want and the lesson I learned long ago about money.

annotations.text: Coming to terms with that fact makes it easier to understand you can’t hold onto your relationship with somebody
